title: The Rhythm of a Crosswalk
text_zh: 每个城市的人行横道都有自己的节奏。在北京，绿灯亮起的时候，人群像决堤的水一样涌出去，步伐快而坚定，因为红灯时间长，大家都憋了一口气。在成都，过马路的速度明显慢一些，走到一半的人可能会因为接了个电话而放慢脚步，后面的人也不着急，绕过去就好了。在上海，人们走得快但有秩序，像一条条平行的溪流，很少有人走出自己的车道。我最喜欢的是一座南方小城的人行横道。那里没有红绿灯，只有地上画的白色斑马线。车让人，人也让车，彼此之间有一种不需要信号灯来调解的默契。有一次我看到一个老人推着自行车过马路，走到一半停下来系鞋带。三辆车安静地等着他，没有人按喇叭。他系完鞋带站起来，朝司机们点了点头，然后不慌不忙地走完了剩下的路。那个场景让我觉得，一个城市的文明程度，不在于它修了多少高楼和地铁，而在于一个老人能不能从容地在马路中间系鞋带。
text_pinyin: Měi gè chéngshì de rénxíng héngdào dōu yǒu zìjǐ de jiézòu. Zài Běijīng, lǜdēng liàng qǐ de shíhou, rénqún xiàng juédī de shuǐ yīyàng yǒng chūqù, bùfá kuài ér jiāndìng, yīnwèi hóngdēng shíjiān cháng, dàjiā dōu biē le yī kǒu qì. Zài Chéngdū, guò mǎlù de sùdù míngxiǎn màn yīxiē, zǒu dào yī bàn de rén kěnéng huì yīnwèi jiē le gè diànhuà ér fàngmàn jiǎobù, hòumian de rén yě bù zhāojí, rào guòqù jiù hǎo le. Zài Shànghǎi, rénmen zǒu de kuài dàn yǒu zhìxù, xiàng yī tiáo tiáo píngxíng de xīliú, hěn shǎo yǒu rén zǒu chū zìjǐ de chēdào. Wǒ zuì xǐhuan de shì yī zuò nánfāng xiǎo chéng de rénxíng héngdào. Nàlǐ méiyǒu hónglǜdēng, zhǐyǒu dì shàng huà de báisè bānmǎxiàn. Chē ràng rén, rén yě ràng chē, bǐcǐ zhī jiān yǒu yī zhǒng bù xūyào xìnhào dēng lái tiáojiě de mòqì. Yǒu yī cì wǒ kàn dào yī gè lǎorén tuī zhe zìxíngchē guò mǎlù, zǒu dào yī bàn tíng xiàlái jì xiédài. Sān liàng chē ānjìng de děng zhe tā, méiyǒu rén àn lǎba. Tā jì wán xiédài zhàn qǐlái, cháo sījīmen diǎn le diǎn tóu, ránhòu bù huāng bù máng de zǒu wán le shèng xià de lù. Nàge chǎngjǐng ràng wǒ juéde, yī gè chéngshì de wénmíng chéngdù, bú zàiyú tā xiū le duōshǎo gāolóu hé dìtiě, ér zàiyú yī gè lǎorén néng bù néng cóngróng de zài mǎlù zhōngjiān jì xiédài.
text_en: Every city's crosswalk has its own rhythm. In Beijing, when the light turns green, the crowd surges out like water breaking through a dam—steps fast and determined, because the red light lasts so long everyone has been holding their breath. In Chengdu, the pace of crossing is noticeably slower; someone halfway across might slow down to answer a phone call, and those behind aren't bothered—they just go around. In Shanghai, people walk fast but orderly, like parallel streams, rarely straying from their lane. My favorite is a crosswalk in a small southern city. There are no traffic lights, just white zebra stripes painted on the ground. Cars yield to people, people yield to cars—between them a mutual understanding that needs no signal light to mediate. Once I watched an elderly man push his bicycle across the street and stop halfway to tie his shoelace. Three cars waited quietly; no one honked. He finished tying his shoe, stood up, nodded to the drivers, then walked the rest of the way without hurrying. That scene made me feel that a city's degree of civilization isn't measured by how many skyscrapers or subway lines it's built, but by whether an old man can calmly tie his shoelace in the middle of the road.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为城市文明程度的标准是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think is the measure of a city's civilization?

options.text_en: Whether an old man can calmly tie his shoelace in the middle of the road

options.pinyin: jiāotōng xìnhào dēng shìfǒu wánshàn
options.text_en: Whether traffic signals are well-equipped

questions.q_zh: 文中提到的南方小城的人行横道有什么特点？
questions.q_en: What is special about the crosswalk in the small southern city?

options.text: 有先进的智能红绿灯系统
options.pinyin: yǒu xiānjìn de zhìnéng hónglǜdēng xìtǒng
options.text_en: It has an advanced smart traffic light system

options.text: 没有红绿灯，靠车和人之间的默契
options.pinyin: méiyǒu hónglǜdēng, kào chē hé rén zhī jiān de mòqì
options.text_en: No traffic lights—it works on mutual understanding between cars and people

options.text_en: Pedestrians cross very quickly

title: The Night Market's Geography
text_zh: 一个城市的夜市不是随便长出来的，它有自己的地理逻辑。通常在老城区的边缘，靠近居民楼但又不至于太近，白天是普通的马路或停车场，到了傍晚开始变形。摊贩们按照一种看不见的秩序排列自己的位置：卖烧烤的集中在上风口，这样烟不会飘到其他摊位上；卖水果的在入口处，因为颜色鲜艳，容易吸引路人停下来；卖衣服和小饰品的在中间段，利用两边食物摊的人流；最里面通常是几家小炒摊和面条摊，它们是夜市的「压轴」，只有走到最深处的人才能发现，但一旦发现就会成为回头客。我曾经连续一个月每晚去同一个夜市，观察它的运作方式。我发现摊贩之间存在着一种微妙的互助关系。卖臭豆腐的老板会帮旁边卖饮料的大姐看摊，因为吃臭豆腐的人一定会买饮料。卖首饰的阿姨会给对面卖煎饼的大哥介绍客人，作为交换，大哥每晚会给她做一份煎饼当晚餐。没有合同，没有协议，只有长年累月形成的默契和信任。夜市是城市最柔软的部分，它证明了即使在商业关系中，人情味也可以是主要的粘合剂。
text_pinyin: Yī gè chéngshì de yèshì bú shì suíbiàn zhǎng chūlái de, tā yǒu zìjǐ de dìlǐ luójí. Tōngcháng zài lǎo chéngqū de biānyuán, kàojìn jūmínlóu dàn yòu bú zhìyú tài jìn, báitiān shì pǔtōng de mǎlù huò tíngchē chǎng, dào le bàngwǎn kāishǐ biànxíng. Tānfàn men ànzhào yī zhǒng kàn bú jiàn de zhìxù páiliè zìjǐ de wèizhì: mài shāokǎo de jízhōng zài shàng fēngkǒu, zhèyàng yān bú huì piāo dào qítā tānwèi shàng; mài shuǐguǒ de zài rùkǒu chù, yīnwèi yánsè xiānyàn, róngyì xīyǐn lùrén tíng xiàlái; mài yīfu hé xiǎo shìpǐn de zài zhōngjiān duàn, lìyòng liǎng biān shíwù tān de rénliú; zuì lǐmiàn tōngcháng shì jǐ jiā xiǎo chǎo tān hé miàntiáo tān, tāmen shì yèshì de「yāzhòu」, zhǐyǒu zǒu dào zuì shēnchù de rén cái néng fāxiàn, dàn yīdàn fāxiàn jiù huì chéngwéi huítóukè. Wǒ céngjīng liánxù yī gè yuè měi wǎn qù tóngyī gè yèshì, guānchá tā de yùnzuò fāngshì. Wǒ fāxiàn tānfàn zhī jiān cúnzài zhe yī zhǒng wēimiào de hùzhù guānxi. Mài chòu dòufu de lǎobǎn huì bāng pángbiān mài yǐnliào de dàjiě kān tān, yīnwèi chī chòu dòufu de rén yīdìng huì mǎi yǐnliào. Mài shǒushì de āyí huì gěi duìmiàn mài jiānbing de dàgē jièshào kèrén, zuòwéi jiāohuàn, dàgē měi wǎn huì gěi tā zuò yī fèn jiānbing dāng wǎncān. Méiyǒu hétong, méiyǒu xiéyì, zhǐyǒu cháng nián lěi yuè xíngchéng de mòqì hé xìnrèn. Yèshì shì chéngshì zuì róuruǎn de bùfen, tā zhèngmíng le jíshǐ zài shāngyè guānxi zhōng, rénqíngwèi yě kěyǐ shì zhǔyào de niánhéjì.
text_en: A city's night market doesn't appear randomly—it has its own geographical logic. Typically at the edge of the old district, near residential buildings but not too close. By day it's an ordinary road or parking lot; at dusk it begins transforming. Vendors arrange themselves according to an invisible order: barbecue stalls cluster upwind so smoke doesn't drift to other stands; fruit sellers station at the entrance because bright colors attract passersby to stop; clothing and accessories vendors occupy the middle section, capitalizing on foot traffic from the food stalls on either side; at the very back are usually a few stir-fry and noodle stands—the night market's 'grand finale,' discovered only by those who venture deepest, but once found they become regulars. I once visited the same night market every evening for a month, observing how it operates. I discovered a subtle web of mutual aid among the vendors. The stinky tofu boss watches the neighboring drink seller's stall, because stinky tofu customers inevitably buy drinks. The accessories auntie refers customers to the pancake vendor across the way, and in exchange he makes her a pancake for dinner each evening. No contracts, no agreements—just understanding and trust built over years. The night market is a city's softest part, proof that even in commercial relationships, human warmth can be the primary adhesive.

questions.q_zh: 夜市摊位的排列有什么规律？
questions.q_en: What pattern governs the arrangement of night market stalls?

title: The Sound Map of a Neighborhood
text_zh: 如果给我住的这个街区画一张声音地图，它大概是这样的：东边是一所小学，每天早上七点半开始有孩子的笑声和读书声，上课铃声在八点整准时响起，像一个声音的分水岭，把喧闹和安静一刀切开。南边是一条商业街，从早到晚都有各种声音——店铺音箱播放的促销广播、外卖骑手的电动车鸣笛、快递员大喊「某某某你的快递到了」。西边是一片老旧的居民区，声音比较碎：锅铲炒菜的声音、电视剧的对白、偶尔从某个窗口飘出来的二胡声——那是退休的赵大爷在练琴。北边是一个小公园，它的声音最有层次：晨练老人的太极拳配乐、鸟叫、风吹过竹林的沙沙声、下午棋友下棋时的争论声。我住在这四种声音区域的交界处，根据听到的声音，不用看钟就大致知道现在是几点。如果听到小学的上课铃声和商业街的开门声同时出现，那就是早上八点。如果听到赵大爷的二胡和公园里的棋友在争论，那大概是下午三四点。声音是城市最真实的脉搏。你可以改变一条街的外观——拆了重建、刷上新漆——但你很难改变它的声音。声音是属于居民的，不属于规划者。
text_pinyin: Rúguǒ gěi wǒ zhù de zhège jiēqū huà yī zhāng shēngyīn dìtú, tā dàgài shì zhèyàng de: dōngbiān shì yī suǒ xiǎoxué, měi tiān zǎoshang qī diǎn bàn kāishǐ yǒu háizi de xiào shēng hé dúshū shēng, shàngkè língshēng zài bā diǎn zhěng zhǔnshí xiǎng qǐ, xiàng yī gè shēngyīn de fēnshuǐlǐng, bǎ xuānnào hé ānjìng yī dāo qiē kāi. Nánbiān shì yī tiáo shāngyè jiē, cóng zǎo dào wǎn dōu yǒu gè zhǒng shēngyīn——diànpù yīnxiāng bōfàng de cùxiāo guǎngbō, wàimài qíshǒu de diàndòngchē míngdí, kuàidì yuán dà hǎn「mǒumǒumǒu nǐ de kuàidì dào le」. Xībiān shì yī piàn lǎojiù de jūmínqū, shēngyīn bǐjiào suì: guōchǎn chǎo cài de shēngyīn, diànshìjù de duìbái, ǒu'ěr cóng mǒu gè chuāngkǒu piāo chūlái de èrhú shēng——nà shì tuìxiū de Zhào dàyé zài liàn qín. Běibiān shì yī gè xiǎo gōngyuán, tā de shēngyīn zuì yǒu céngcì: chénliàn lǎorén de tàijíquán pèiyuè, niǎo jiào, fēng chuī guò zhúlín de shāshā shēng, xiàwǔ qíyǒu xià qí shí de zhēnglùn shēng. Wǒ zhù zài zhè sì zhǒng shēngyīn qūyù de jiāojièchù, gēnjù tīng dào de shēngyīn, búyòng kàn zhōng jiù dàzhì zhīdào xiànzài shì jǐ diǎn. Rúguǒ tīng dào xiǎoxué de shàngkè língshēng hé shāngyè jiē de kāimén shēng tóngshí chūxiàn, nà jiùshì zǎoshang bā diǎn. Rúguǒ tīng dào Zhào dàyé de èrhú hé gōngyuán lǐ de qíyǒu zài zhēnglùn, nà dàgài shì xiàwǔ sān sì diǎn. Shēngyīn shì chéngshì zuì zhēnshí de màibó. Nǐ kěyǐ gǎibiàn yī tiáo jiē de wàiguān——chāi le chóngjiàn, shuā shàng xīn qī——dàn nǐ hěn nán gǎibiàn tā de shēngyīn. Shēngyīn shì shǔyú jūmín de, bù shǔyú guīhuà zhě.
text_en: If I were to draw a sound map of my neighborhood, it would look roughly like this: to the east is an elementary school—every morning at seven-thirty come children's laughter and reading aloud; the class bell rings at exactly eight, like an acoustic watershed cleaving noise from silence. To the south runs a commercial street, noisy from dawn to dusk—shop speakers blasting promotions, delivery riders honking electric scooters, couriers shouting 'So-and-so, your package is here!' To the west lies an old residential area with fragmented sounds: the clang of wok spatulas stir-frying, TV drama dialogue, and occasionally from some window the drifting notes of an erhu—retired Grandpa Zhao practicing his instrument. To the north is a small park with the most layered soundscape: tai chi music for morning exercisers, birdsong, the rustle of wind through bamboo groves, and afternoon arguments between chess partners. I live at the junction of these four sound zones and can roughly tell the time by what I hear, without looking at a clock. If the school bell and the commercial street's door-opening sounds appear simultaneously, it's eight a.m. If I hear Grandpa Zhao's erhu and the park chess players arguing, it's around three or four p.m. Sound is a city's truest pulse. You can change a street's appearance—tear it down, rebuild, slap on fresh paint—but you can hardly change its sound. Sound belongs to the residents, not to the planners.

questions.q_zh: 作者怎样不看钟就知道时间？
questions.q_en: How does the author tell time without looking at a clock?

options.text: 通过周围不同区域的声音组合来判断
options.pinyin: tōngguò zhōuwéi bùtóng qūyù de shēngyīn zǔhé lái pànduàn
options.text_en: By judging from the combination of sounds from different surrounding areas

options.pinyin: tōngguò tàiyáng de wèizhì
options.text_en: By the position of the sun

options.pinyin: tōngguò jiē shàng rén de duōshǎo
options.text_en: By the number of people on the street

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么说「声音是属于居民的，不属于规划者」？
questions.q_en: Why does the author say 'sound belongs to the residents, not to the planners'?

options.text: 因为声音是居民日常生活自然产生的，无法被规划改变
options.pinyin: yīnwèi shēngyīn shì jūmín rìcháng shēnghuó zìrán chǎnshēng de, wúfǎ bèi guīhuà gǎibiàn
options.text_en: Because sound is naturally produced by residents' daily life and can't be changed by planning

options.text: 因为规划者不关心噪音问题
options.pinyin: yīnwèi guīhuà zhě bù guānxīn zàoyīn wèntí
options.text_en: Because planners don't care about noise issues

options.text: 因为居民会抗议城市改造
options.pinyin: yīnwèi jūmín huì kàngyì chéngshì gǎizào
options.text_en: Because residents protest urban renovation

title: The Corner Store That Never Changes
text_zh: 我家楼下的街角有一家小卖部，开了至少二十年。这些年来，周围的一切都变了：隔壁的理发店变成了奶茶店，对面的书报亭消失了，马路对面盖起了一栋商业中心。但这家小卖部还在，而且几乎没有变过。同样的玻璃柜台，同样的塑料凳子，同样的货架上摆着同样品牌的矿泉水、方便面和老式汽水。老板还是那个姓刘的大叔，只是头发白了一些，肚子大了一圈。我有时候觉得这家小卖部不是一个商店，而是一个时间锚点。当你从外面的世界回来，被各种变化搅得有点晕的时候，看到它还在那里，还是老样子，心里就会踏实一些。就像每个人心里都需要一个不会变的东西，来证明自己的记忆没有出错。有一次我跟刘大叔聊天，问他为什么不装修一下，也卖点时髦的东西。他想了想说：「我这种店就是给不想做选择的人准备的。他们下楼来买瓶水，不想面对二十种选择，就想要那种他们从小喝到大的，拿了就走。」我听完觉得他说得很对。在一个选择越来越多的世界里，不给你选择，本身就是一种服务。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā lóu xià de jiējiǎo yǒu yī jiā xiǎomàibù, kāi le zhìshǎo èrshí nián. Zhèxiē nián lái, zhōuwéi de yīqiè dōu biàn le: gébì de lǐfǎdiàn biànchéng le nǎichá diàn, duìmiàn de shūbàotíng xiāoshī le, mǎlù duìmiàn gài qǐ le yī dòng shāngyè zhōngxīn. Dàn zhè jiā xiǎomàibù hái zài, érqiě jīhū méiyǒu biàn guò. Tóngyàng de bōli guìtái, tóngyàng de sùliào dèngzi, tóngyàng de huòjià shàng bǎi zhe tóngyàng pǐnpái de kuàngquánshuǐ, fāngbiànmiàn hé lǎoshì qìshuǐ. Lǎobǎn háishi nàge xìng Liú de dàshū, zhǐshì tóufa bái le yīxiē, dùzi dà le yī quān. Wǒ yǒu shíhou juéde zhè jiā xiǎomàibù bú shì yī gè shāngdiàn, ér shì yī gè shíjiān máodiǎn. Dāng nǐ cóng wàimiàn de shìjiè huílái, bèi gè zhǒng biànhuà jiǎo de yǒudiǎn yūn de shíhou, kàn dào tā hái zài nàlǐ, háishi lǎo yàngzi, xīnlǐ jiù huì tāshi yīxiē. Jiù xiàng měi gè rén xīnlǐ dōu xūyào yī gè bú huì biàn de dōngxi, lái zhèngmíng zìjǐ de jìyì méiyǒu chū cuò. Yǒu yī cì wǒ gēn Liú dàshū liáotiān, wèn tā wèishéme bù zhuāngxiū yīxià, yě mài diǎn shímáo de dōngxi. Tā xiǎng le xiǎng shuō: 「Wǒ zhè zhǒng diàn jiùshì gěi bù xiǎng zuò xuǎnzé de rén zhǔnbèi de. Tāmen xià lóu lái mǎi píng shuǐ, bù xiǎng miànduì èrshí zhǒng xuǎnzé, jiù xiǎng yào nà zhǒng tāmen cóng xiǎo hē dào dà de, ná le jiù zǒu.」Wǒ tīng wán juéde tā shuō de hěn duì. Zài yī gè xuǎnzé yuè lái yuè duō de shìjiè lǐ, bù gěi nǐ xuǎnzé, běnshēn jiùshì yī zhǒng fúwù.
text_en: On the corner downstairs from my apartment is a convenience store that's been open at least twenty years. Everything around it has changed over the years: the barbershop next door became a milk tea shop, the newsstand across the way disappeared, a commercial center rose on the opposite side of the road. But this little store remains, virtually unchanged. Same glass counter, same plastic stools, same shelf brands of mineral water, instant noodles, and old-fashioned soda. The owner is still that Uncle Liu, just with somewhat whiter hair and a slightly larger belly. Sometimes I feel this store isn't a shop but a temporal anchor point. When you come back from the outside world, a bit dizzy from all the changes, seeing it still there, looking the same, settles your heart. It's as if everyone needs one unchanging thing to prove that their memory hasn't gone wrong. Once I chatted with Uncle Liu and asked why he didn't renovate and sell some trendy products. He thought about it and said: 'My kind of store is for people who don't want to make choices. They come downstairs to buy a bottle of water, don't want to face twenty options—they just want the kind they've been drinking since childhood, grab it and go.' I thought he was absolutely right. In a world with ever more choices, not giving you choices is itself a kind of service.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么把这家小卖部比作「时间锚点」？
questions.q_en: Why does the author compare this store to a 'temporal anchor point'?

options.text: 因为它在变化的环境中保持不变，让人心里踏实
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā zài biànhuà de huánjìng zhōng bǎochí bú biàn, ràng rén xīnlǐ tāshi
options.text_en: Because it stays unchanged in a changing environment, giving people a sense of stability

options.text: 因为它卖的都是老式商品
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā mài de dōu shì lǎoshì shāngpǐn
options.text_en: Because it sells old-fashioned products

options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā wèiyú jiējiǎo
options.text_en: Because it's located on the corner

questions.q_zh: 刘大叔为什么不做任何改变？
questions.q_en: Why doesn't Uncle Liu make any changes?

options.pinyin: tā méiyǒu qián zhuāngxiū
options.text_en: He doesn't have money to renovate

options.text: 他的店是为不想做选择的人服务的
options.pinyin: tā de diàn shì wèi bù xiǎng zuò xuǎnzé de rén fúwù de
options.text_en: His store serves people who don't want to make choices

options.pinyin: tā jíjiāng tuìxiū
options.text_en: He's about to retire

title: The Bus Stop at Midnight
text_zh: 晚上十一点以后的公交车站是一个完全不同的空间。白天它是一个功能性的存在——人们来了又走，看站牌、查手机、等车、上车。但深夜的公交站变成了一个收容所，收容着所有还没有回家的人。等末班车的人很少说话。他们各自坐在候车椅上，或者靠着站牌柱子，做着各自的事。有人在看手机，屏幕的蓝光照着一张疲惫的脸。有人在抽烟，烟头的红点在夜里一明一灭。有人只是闭着眼睛，但不是在睡觉，更像是在屏蔽一天的噪音。我注意到一个有趣的现象：深夜的公交站里，人和人之间的距离会自动拉近。白天排队等车，人与人保持至少一臂的距离。但深夜的时候，两个陌生人可能会并肩坐在同一张椅子上，不交谈，但也不介意对方的存在。也许是因为深夜的城市让人感到微弱的不安全感，陌生人的存在反而变成了一种慰藉。「我不是唯一一个还没回家的人」——这个事实本身就够温暖了。末班车终于来了的时候，所有人都站起来，像一个临时的社团完成了它的使命。明天他们不会在白天的公交站认出彼此。午夜的连接，只属于午夜。
text_pinyin: Wǎnshang shíyī diǎn yǐhòu de gōngjiāo chēzhàn shì yī gè wánquán bùtóng de kōngjiān. Báitiān tā shì yī gè gōngnéng xìng de cúnzài——rénmen lái le yòu zǒu, kàn zhànpái, chá shǒujī, děng chē, shàng chē. Dàn shēnyè de gōngjiāo zhàn biànchéng le yī gè shōuróng suǒ, shōuróng zhe suǒyǒu hái méiyǒu huí jiā de rén. Děng mòbān chē de rén hěn shǎo shuōhuà. Tāmen gèzì zuò zài hòuchē yǐ shàng, huòzhě kào zhe zhànpái zhùzi, zuò zhe gèzì de shì. Yǒu rén zài kàn shǒujī, píngmù de lán guāng zhào zhe yī zhāng píbèi de liǎn. Yǒu rén zài chōuyān, yāntóu de hóng diǎn zài yè lǐ yī míng yī miè. Yǒu rén zhǐshì bì zhe yǎnjīng, dàn bú shì zài shuìjiào, gèng xiàng shì zài píngbì yī tiān de zàoyīn. Wǒ zhùyì dào yī gè yǒuqù de xiànxiàng: shēnyè de gōngjiāo zhàn lǐ, rén hé rén zhī jiān de jùlí huì zìdòng lā jìn. Báitiān páiduì děng chē, rén yǔ rén bǎochí zhìshǎo yī bì de jùlí. Dàn shēnyè de shíhou, liǎng gè mòshēng rén kěnéng huì bìngjiān zuò zài tóng yī zhāng yǐzi shàng, bù jiāotán, dàn yě bú jièyì duìfāng de cúnzài. Yěxǔ shì yīnwèi shēnyè de chéngshì ràng rén gǎndào wēiruò de bù ānquán gǎn, mòshēng rén de cúnzài fǎn'ér biànchéng le yī zhǒng wèijiè. 「Wǒ bú shì wéiyī yī gè hái méi huí jiā de rén」——zhège shìshí běnshēn jiù gòu wēnnuǎn le. Mòbān chē zhōngyú lái le de shíhou, suǒyǒu rén dōu zhàn qǐlái, xiàng yī gè línshí de shètuán wánchéng le tā de shǐmìng. Míngtiān tāmen bú huì zài báitiān de gōngjiāo zhàn rèn chū bǐcǐ. Wǔyè de liánjiē, zhǐ shǔyú wǔyè.
text_en: After eleven p.m., a bus stop becomes a completely different space. By day it's purely functional—people come and go, check the route sign, look at phones, wait, board. But at night the bus stop transforms into a shelter, harboring everyone who hasn't yet gone home. People waiting for the last bus rarely speak. They each sit on the waiting bench or lean against the stop's pillar, doing their own thing. Someone scrolls their phone, the screen's blue glow illuminating a weary face. Someone smokes, the cigarette's red tip flickering in the darkness. Someone simply closes their eyes—not sleeping, more like muting the day's noise. I noticed an interesting phenomenon: at a late-night bus stop, the distance between people naturally shrinks. During the day, people queuing for a bus maintain at least an arm's length. But late at night, two strangers might sit shoulder to shoulder on the same bench, not talking but not minding each other's presence. Perhaps the city at night creates a faint sense of insecurity, and a stranger's presence becomes solace instead. 'I'm not the only one who hasn't gone home'—that fact alone is warm enough. When the last bus finally arrives, everyone stands up, like a temporary society that has fulfilled its mission. Tomorrow they won't recognize each other at the daytime bus stop. Midnight's connection belongs only to midnight.

questions.q_zh: 深夜的公交站和白天相比有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How is the late-night bus stop different from the daytime one?

options.pinyin: rén gèng duō le
options.text_en: There are more people

options.text: 人与人之间的距离自动拉近，陌生人可以并肩而坐
options.pinyin: rén yǔ rén zhī jiān de jùlí zìdòng lā jìn, mòshēng rén kěyǐ bìngjiān ér zuò
options.text_en: The distance between people naturally shrinks; strangers can sit shoulder to shoulder

options.pinyin: rénmen gèng xǐhuan jiāotán
options.text_en: People are more likely to chat

questions.q_zh: 「午夜的连接只属于午夜」是什么意思？
questions.q_en: What does 'midnight's connection belongs only to midnight' mean?

options.text: 深夜陌生人之间的联系是短暂的，白天不会延续
options.pinyin: shēnyè mòshēng rén zhī jiān de liánxì shì duǎnzàn de, báitiān bú huì yánxù
options.text_en: The bond between strangers at night is brief and doesn't continue by day

options.text: 午夜的公交车比白天的更方便
options.pinyin: wǔyè de gōngjiāo chē bǐ báitiān de gèng fāngbiàn
options.text_en: Midnight buses are more convenient than daytime ones

options.pinyin: zhǐyǒu wǔyè cái yǒu mòbān chē
options.text_en: Only midnight has the last bus

title: The Alley Cat's Territory
text_zh: 我住的这条巷子里有大约七八只流浪猫。经过长时间的观察，我发现它们对这条巷子进行了非常精确的领地划分。一只橘猫占据了巷口到第一个垃圾桶之间的区域，那里经过的行人最多，投食的概率最高。一只黑白花猫控制着两栋居民楼之间的夹缝，那里狭窄但安全，下雨也淋不到。一只灰色老猫盘踞在巷子尽头的一个废弃花坛上，居高临下，适合睡觉和监视整条巷子。这些领地的边界是流动的、模糊的，但猫们都心知肚明。偶尔会有越界事件发生——通常是新来的猫不了解规矩，走进了别人的地盘。这时候原住民会发出低沉的警告声，不是立刻攻击，而是给对方一个退出的机会。大部分时候，入侵者会识趣地退回去。我观察到最有意思的一幕是在下雨天。当暴雨来临，所有的领地概念都暂时失效了。猫们会挤在同一个屋檐下，彼此之间保持着最小的距离，谁也不发出威胁的声音。等雨一停，它们立刻各自回到自己的领地，仿佛刚才的亲密不曾发生。这跟人类社会何其相似——危机让我们团结，安全让我们划清界限。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù de zhè tiáo xiàngzi lǐ yǒu dàyuē qī bā zhī liúlàng māo. Jīngguò cháng shíjiān de guānchá, wǒ fāxiàn tāmen duì zhè tiáo xiàngzi jìnxíng le fēicháng jīngquè de lǐngdì huáfēn. Yī zhī jú māo zhànjù le xiàng kǒu dào dì yī gè lājī tǒng zhī jiān de qūyù, nàlǐ jīngguò de xíngrén zuì duō, tóu shí de gàilǜ zuì gāo. Yī zhī hēi bái huā māo kòngzhì zhe liǎng dòng jūmínlóu zhī jiān de jiāfèng, nàlǐ xiázhǎi dàn ānquán, xià yǔ yě lín bú dào. Yī zhī huīsè lǎo māo pánjù zài xiàngzi jìntóu de yī gè fèiqì huātán shàng, jūgāolínxià, shìhé shuìjiào hé jiānshì zhěng tiáo xiàngzi. Zhèxiē lǐngdì de biānjiè shì liúdòng de, móhu de, dàn māomen dōu xīnzhī dùmíng. Ǒu'ěr huì yǒu yuèjiè shìjiàn fāshēng——tōngcháng shì xīn lái de māo bù liǎojiě guīju, zǒu jìn le biéren de dìpán. Zhè shíhou yuánzhùmín huì fāchū dīchén de jǐnggào shēng, bú shì lìkè gōngjī, ér shì gěi duìfāng yī gè tuìchū de jīhuì. Dà bùfen shíhou, qīnlüè zhě huì shíqù de tuì huíqù. Wǒ guānchá dào zuì yǒu yìsi de yī mù shì zài xià yǔ tiān. Dāng bàoyǔ láilín, suǒyǒu de lǐngdì gàiniàn dōu zànshí shīxiào le. Māomen huì jǐ zài tóng yī gè wūyán xià, bǐcǐ zhī jiān bǎochí zhe zuìxiǎo de jùlí, shéi yě bù fāchū wēixié de shēngyīn. Děng yǔ yī tíng, tāmen lìkè gèzì huí dào zìjǐ de lǐngdì, fǎngfú gāngcái de qīnmì bù céng fāshēng. Zhè gēn rénlèi shèhuì héqí xiāngsì——wēijī ràng wǒmen tuánjié, ānquán ràng wǒmen huà qīng jièxiàn.
text_en: There are roughly seven or eight stray cats in my alley. After long observation, I discovered they've partitioned it with remarkable precision. An orange cat occupies the zone from the alley entrance to the first trash bin—the highest foot traffic and best odds of being fed. A black-and-white cat controls the narrow gap between two residential buildings—tight but safe, sheltered from rain. An old gray cat perches atop an abandoned flower bed at the alley's end—elevated, ideal for sleeping and surveilling the entire lane. These territorial boundaries are fluid and blurry, but the cats all know them by heart. Occasionally there are border crossings—usually a newcomer unfamiliar with the rules wandering into someone else's turf. The resident will issue a low warning sound—not an immediate attack, but giving the other a chance to withdraw. Most of the time, the intruder wisely retreats. The most interesting scene I observed was during rain. When a downpour arrives, all territorial concepts temporarily collapse. The cats squeeze under the same eave, maintaining minimal distance from each other, no one making threatening sounds. The moment the rain stops, they immediately return to their own territories, as if the closeness never happened. How similar this is to human society—crisis unites us; safety makes us draw clear boundaries.

questions.q_zh: 橘猫为什么占据巷口到垃圾桶之间的区域？
questions.q_en: Why does the orange cat occupy the area from the alley entrance to the trash bin?

options.pinyin: nàlǐ zuì nuǎnhuo
options.text_en: It's the warmest spot

options.text: 那里行人最多，被投食的概率最高
options.pinyin: nàlǐ xíngrén zuì duō, bèi tóu shí de gàilǜ zuì gāo
options.text_en: Most pedestrians pass there, giving the highest chance of being fed

options.pinyin: nàlǐ yǒu shù kěyǐ pá
options.text_en: There are trees to climb

questions.q_zh: 下雨天猫的行为跟人类社会有什么共同点？
questions.q_en: What do the cats' rainy-day behavior and human society have in common?

options.text: 危机让彼此团结，安全后又划清界限
options.pinyin: wēijī ràng bǐcǐ tuánjié, ānquán hòu yòu huà qīng jièxiàn
options.text_en: Crisis brings unity; safety restores boundaries

options.pinyin: xià yǔ tiān dàjiā dōu bù chūmén
options.text_en: Nobody goes out when it rains

options.pinyin: yǔ tiān dàjiā gèng yǒuhǎo
options.text_en: Everyone is friendlier on rainy days

title: The Elevator's Sociology
text_zh: 我住在十八楼，每天要坐电梯上下好几次。住久了之后，我发现电梯是一个极其精妙的社会学实验室。首先是站位。当电梯里只有一个人的时候，他通常站在按钮面板旁边，面对着门。当第二个人进来，第一个人会不自觉地移到角落，把中间的空间让出来。当第三个、第四个人进来，每个人都会自动寻找离其他人最远的位置，像磁铁的同极在互相排斥。然后是目光。电梯里的人几乎从来不互相看。他们看手机、看楼层显示屏、看天花板、看地面——就是不看对面的人。这是一种无声的社交协议：「我们暂时被困在这个狭小的空间里，但我保证不侵犯你的个人空间。」最有趣的是声音的变化。一个人独自在电梯里可能会哼歌、自言自语甚至小声放屁。两个人的时候就变安静了。三个人以上，整个电梯会陷入一种几乎令人不舒服的沉默。但偶尔，有一种声音能打破这种沉默——当电梯突然晃了一下或者发出异常的响声，所有人会同时抬头，互相交换一个「怎么回事」的眼神。在那一瞬间，陌生人之间的屏障消失了，因为共同的不安全感把他们暂时变成了同盟。然后电梯恢复正常运行，屏障重新升起。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù zài shíbā lóu, měi tiān yào zuò diàntī shàng xià hǎo jǐ cì. Zhù jiǔ le zhīhòu, wǒ fāxiàn diàntī shì yī gè jíqí jīngmiào de shèhuì xué shíyànshì. Shǒuxiān shì zhànwèi. Dāng diàntī lǐ zhǐyǒu yī gè rén de shíhou, tā tōngcháng zhàn zài ànniǔ miànbǎn pángbiān, miànduì zhe mén. Dāng dì èr gè rén jìnlái, dì yī gè rén huì bù zìjué de yí dào jiǎoluò, bǎ zhōngjiān de kōngjiān ràng chūlái. Dāng dì sān gè, dì sì gè rén jìnlái, měi gè rén dōu huì zìdòng xúnzhǎo lí qítā rén zuì yuǎn de wèizhì, xiàng cítiě de tóng jí zài hùxiāng páichì. Ránhòu shì mùguāng. Diàntī lǐ de rén jīhū cónglái bù hùxiāng kàn. Tāmen kàn shǒujī, kàn lóucéng xiǎnshìpíng, kàn tiānhuābǎn, kàn dìmiàn——jiùshì bú kàn duìmiàn de rén. Zhè shì yī zhǒng wúshēng de shèjiāo xiéyì: 「Wǒmen zànshí bèi kùn zài zhège xiáxiǎo de kōngjiān lǐ, dàn wǒ bǎozhèng bù qīnfàn nǐ de gèrén kōngjiān.」Zuì yǒuqù de shì shēngyīn de biànhuà. Yī gè rén dúzì zài diàntī lǐ kěnéng huì hēng gē, zìyán zìyǔ shènzhì xiǎo shēng fàng pì. Liǎng gè rén de shíhou jiù biàn ānjìng le. Sān gè rén yǐshàng, zhěnggè diàntī huì xiàn rù yī zhǒng jīhū lìng rén bù shūfu de chénmò. Dàn ǒu'ěr, yǒu yī zhǒng shēngyīn néng dǎpò zhè zhǒng chénmò——dāng diàntī tūrán huàng le yīxià huòzhě fāchū yìcháng de xiǎngshēng, suǒyǒu rén huì tóngshí tái tóu, hùxiāng jiāohuàn yī gè「zěnme huí shì」de yǎnshén. Zài nà yī shùnjiān, mòshēng rén zhī jiān de píngzhàng xiāoshī le, yīnwèi gòngtóng de bù ānquán gǎn bǎ tāmen zànshí biànchéng le tóngméng. Ránhòu diàntī huīfù zhèngcháng yùnxíng, píngzhàng chóngxīn shēng qǐ.
text_en: I live on the eighteenth floor and take the elevator several times daily. After living here long enough, I discovered the elevator is an exquisitely precise sociology laboratory. First, positioning. When only one person is in the elevator, they usually stand by the button panel, facing the door. When a second person enters, the first unconsciously shifts to a corner, vacating the middle. When a third and fourth enter, each automatically seeks the position farthest from everyone else, like same-polarity magnets repelling each other. Then, gaze. People in elevators almost never look at each other. They look at phones, floor displays, the ceiling, the floor—just not at the person opposite. This is an unspoken social contract: 'We're temporarily trapped in this small space, but I promise not to invade your personal space.' Most interesting is the change in sound. Alone in an elevator, a person might hum, talk to themselves, even quietly pass gas. With two people, silence descends. With three or more, the entire elevator falls into an almost uncomfortable hush. But occasionally, one sound can break this silence—when the elevator suddenly shakes or makes an unusual noise, everyone simultaneously looks up, exchanging a 'what's going on?' glance. In that instant, the barrier between strangers vanishes, because shared insecurity temporarily turns them into allies. Then the elevator resumes normal operation, and the barrier rises again.

questions.q_zh: 电梯里人越多时，人们的行为有什么变化？
questions.q_en: How does behavior change as more people enter the elevator?

options.pinyin: rénmen biàn de gèng yǒuhǎo
options.text_en: People become friendlier

options.text: 每个人自动寻找离他人最远的位置，避免目光接触，保持沉默
options.pinyin: měi gè rén zìdòng xúnzhǎo lí tārén zuì yuǎn de wèizhì, bìmiǎn mùguāng jiēchù, bǎochí chénmò
options.text_en: Everyone automatically finds the farthest position, avoids eye contact, and stays silent

options.pinyin: rénmen kāishǐ hùxiāng jiāotán
options.text_en: People start talking to each other

questions.q_zh: 什么情况能让电梯里的陌生人之间的屏障暂时消失？
questions.q_en: What situation can temporarily dissolve the barrier between elevator strangers?

options.pinyin: dāng yǒu rén kāishǐ shuōhuà
options.text_en: When someone starts talking

options.text: 当电梯突然晃动或发出异常声响
options.pinyin: dāng diàntī tūrán huàngdòng huò fāchū yìcháng shēngxiǎng
options.text_en: When the elevator suddenly shakes or makes an unusual sound

options.pinyin: dāng diàntī dàodá mùbiāo lóucéng
options.text_en: When the elevator reaches the destination floor

title: Rain and the City's True Face
text_zh: 一场大雨能让一个城市暴露出它平时藏起来的一切。路面上的裂缝平时看不出来，积水之后就成了一个个小水坑，告诉你哪些路段的施工偷了工减了料。下水道的设计好不好，平时根本感觉不到，暴雨一来就知道了——如果某条街总是淹水，说明当初的排水系统没有认真计算过流量。但雨水暴露的不仅仅是基础设施的问题。它还暴露了人的性格。我看过各种各样的人在突然的暴雨中的反应。有人立刻跑向最近的屋檐下，用包顶在头上，表情焦虑，好像被雨淋到是一种极大的冒犯。有人不慌不忙地继续走，衣服湿了也不在意，脸上甚至带着一种释然的表情——好像他一直在等这场雨来解放他。有人站在路边犹豫不决，不知道该跑还是该等，最后选择了一个折中方案：小跑。还有一种人是我最佩服的：他们会慢下来，抬头看雨，然后微笑。不是苦笑，不是无奈的笑，是真正的欣赏的微笑。他们似乎明白一件事：你已经在雨里了，慌张也不能让你变干。既然如此，不如享受它。这大概就是所谓的「从容」。从容不是一种性格，而是一种决定——决定不再跟无法控制的事情对抗。
text_pinyin: Yī chǎng dà yǔ néng ràng yī gè chéngshì bàolù chū tā píngshí cáng qǐlái de yīqiè. Lùmiàn shàng de lièfèng píngshí kàn bù chūlái, jīshuǐ zhīhòu jiù chéng le yī gè gè xiǎo shuǐkēng, gàosu nǐ nǎxiē lùduàn de shīgōng tōu le gōng jiǎn le liào. Xiàshuǐdào de shèjì hǎo bù hǎo, píngshí gēnběn gǎnjué bú dào, bàoyǔ yī lái jiù zhīdào le——rúguǒ mǒu tiáo jiē zǒng shì yān shuǐ, shuōmíng dāngchū de páishuǐ xìtǒng méiyǒu rènzhēn jìsuàn guò liúliàng. Dàn yǔshuǐ bàolù de bùjǐnjǐn shì jīchǔ shèshī de wèntí. Tā hái bàolù le rén de xìnggé. Wǒ kàn guò gè zhǒng gè yàng de rén zài tūrán de bàoyǔ zhōng de fǎnyìng. Yǒu rén lìkè pǎo xiàng zuì jìn de wūyán xià, yòng bāo dǐng zài tóu shàng, biǎoqíng jiāolǜ, hǎoxiàng bèi yǔ lín dào shì yī zhǒng jí dà de màofàn. Yǒu rén bù huāng bù máng de jìxù zǒu, yīfu shī le yě bú zàiyì, liǎn shàng shènzhì dài zhe yī zhǒng shìrán de biǎoqíng——hǎoxiàng tā yīzhí zài děng zhè chǎng yǔ lái jiěfàng tā. Yǒu rén zhàn zài lù biān yóuyù bù jué, bù zhīdào gāi pǎo háishi gāi děng, zuìhòu xuǎnzé le yī gè zhézhōng fāng'àn: xiǎo pǎo. Hái yǒu yī zhǒng rén shì wǒ zuì pèifú de: tāmen huì màn xiàlái, tái tóu kàn yǔ, ránhòu wēixiào. Bú shì kǔxiào, bú shì wúnài de xiào, shì zhēnzhèng de xīnshǎng de wēixiào. Tāmen sìhū míngbai yī jiàn shì: nǐ yǐjīng zài yǔ lǐ le, huāngzhāng yě bù néng ràng nǐ biàn gān. Jìrán rúcǐ, bùrú xiǎngshòu tā. Zhè dàgài jiùshì suǒwèi de「cóngróng」. Cóngróng bú shì yī zhǒng xìnggé, ér shì yī zhǒng juédìng——juédìng bú zài gēn wúfǎ kòngzhì de shìqing duìkàng.
text_en: A heavy rain can make a city reveal everything it normally hides. Cracks in the road surface are invisible until water pools into puddles, telling you which sections were shoddily constructed. Whether the drainage system is well-designed goes completely unnoticed until a downpour comes—if a certain street always floods, the original drainage system never properly calculated the flow. But rain exposes more than infrastructure problems. It also exposes character. I've watched all kinds of people react to sudden downpours. Some immediately dash to the nearest eave, holding bags over their heads, faces anxious, as if getting rained on is a grave offense. Others continue unhurriedly, not minding wet clothes, faces even carrying a look of relief—as if they'd been waiting for this rain to liberate them. Some stand at the roadside indecisive, unsure whether to run or wait, finally choosing a compromise: a light jog. Then there's the type I most admire: they slow down, look up at the rain, and smile. Not a wry smile, not a helpless one, but a genuine smile of appreciation. They seem to understand something: you're already in the rain—panicking won't make you dry. Since that's the case, you might as well enjoy it. This is probably what's called 'composure.' Composure isn't a personality trait but a decision—a decision to stop fighting what you can't control.

questions.q_zh: 作者最佩服的是哪种人在暴雨中的反应？
questions.q_en: Which type of rain reaction does the author most admire?

options.pinyin: lìkè pǎo xiàng wūyán xià de rén
options.text_en: Those who immediately dash to shelter

options.text: 慢下来，抬头看雨，然后真正欣赏地微笑的人
options.pinyin: màn xiàlái, tái tóu kàn yǔ, ránhòu zhēnzhèng xīnshǎng de wēixiào de rén
options.text_en: Those who slow down, look up at the rain, and smile with genuine appreciation

options.pinyin: bù huāng bù máng jìxù zǒu de rén
options.text_en: Those who continue walking unhurriedly

questions.q_zh: 作者认为「从容」的本质是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author think is the essence of 'composure'?

options.pinyin: yī zhǒng tiānshēng de xìnggé tèzhēng
options.text_en: An innate personality trait

options.text: 一种决定——不再跟无法控制的事情对抗
options.pinyin: yī zhǒng juédìng——bú zài gēn wúfǎ kòngzhì de shìqing duìkàng
options.text_en: A decision—to stop fighting what you can't control

options.text: 一种通过练习获得的技能
options.pinyin: yī zhǒng tōngguò liànxí huòdé de jìnéng
options.text_en: A skill acquired through practice
